zones.name: .rex
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
These files are generated artifacts, not source code — no entry points or cross-zone imports is correct and expected behavior.
The prd.json and execution-log.jsonl files are the source of truth for task state; treat them as the system's persistent store, not as editable source.
Keeping these files in a dedicated directory isolates mutable runtime state from the source tree, which is good practice for reproducibility.
All four files are generated data artifacts (JSON, JSONL, Markdown). Perfect cohesion and zero coupling confirm this zone is correctly isolated from the source graph.

zones.name: Cli
insights: High coupling (0.53) — 58 imports target "unit-analyze"
13 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports
High coupling (0.53) — 58 imports target "prd-analysis-core"
13 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports

13 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports

The bidirectional import relationship with prd-analysis-core (58 outbound, 35 inbound) is an architectural red flag — CLI should depend on domain, but domain importing from CLI suggests shared utilities or types that should be extracted to a neutral foundation layer.
The chunked-review-state.ts alongside chunked-review.ts indicates stateful multi-step workflows in the CLI; ensure this state is not leaking into the domain layer through the reverse imports.

The co-location of mcp.ts with CLI command infrastructure is sensible — MCP is another transport layer for the same domain operations — but verify the MCP handler delegates to the same domain functions as the CLI rather than duplicating logic.

Reverse imports from prd-analysis-core back into the cli zone (35 imports) break the expected CLI→Domain dependency direction. Extract any shared utilities or types into a neutral module to restore layering.
Token usage tracking (token-usage.ts, token-format.ts) co-located with command routing and MCP infrastructure is a natural fit — these are all cross-cutting CLI concerns rather than domain logic.
Zone "cli-mcp-interface" has files across 9 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

cli-mcp-interface sends imports to three zones (unit-analyze: 58, core: 36, store: 21) while receiving from all three back (35+21+3), forming a three-way bidirectional entanglement rather than a single isolated coupling pair.

The 21-import path cli → store combined with store → unit-analyze (22) creates a transitive chain that duplicates the direct cli → unit-analyze (58) path — two routes to the same destination suggest the abstraction boundaries between these three zones are not meaningful at runtime.

cli-mcp-interface participates in bidirectional coupling with three separate zones simultaneously (prd-analysis-core, core, store) — the CLI layer has no clean downstream; it is structurally merged with the entire domain.

cli → store (21) + store → prd-analysis-core (22) creates a parallel transitive path alongside the direct cli → prd-analysis-core (58) route, indicating the store abstraction boundary is not enforced — consumers bypass it freely.

reshape-reason.ts is physically in src/analyze/ and logically depends only on analysis/core internals, yet Louvain assigned it to this zone — its presence here is an artifact of import-graph community detection noise, not a deliberate architectural decision.

src/analyze/reshape-reason.ts is misclassified in the CLI zone: it lives in src/analyze/, imports from reason.ts and core/reshape.ts, and contains no CLI or transport concerns — move it to prd-analysis-core.

chunked-review-state.ts implements a manually-built state machine for multi-step interactive workflows with no shared abstraction — if any other command ever requires multi-step stateful interaction, this pattern will be re-implemented ad-hoc rather than reused, suggesting a StatefulCommand or WorkflowStep interface should be extracted.

Token telemetry (token-usage.ts, token-format.ts) lives exclusively in the CLI zone, meaning domain operations have zero visibility into token consumption — it is architecturally impossible to enforce token budgets or emit token telemetry from domain-layer analysis operations without first moving telemetry to a lower layer.

cli-mcp-interface has cohesion 0.49 and coupling 0.51, both crossing the warning threshold simultaneously like recommendation-scanning — these are the two fragile zones; all other source zones have at least one metric on the safe side.

cli-mcp-interface shares the dual-threshold fragility pattern with recommendation-scanning (cohesion below 0.5, coupling above 0.5 simultaneously). Both zones should be addressed in the same refactoring pass since they are structurally linked via bidirectional imports.

Token telemetry is CLI-layer-only. Move token consumption tracking to a cross-cutting concern at the llm-client foundation layer so domain analysis operations can emit telemetry without depending on CLI infrastructure.

chunked-review-state.ts is a one-off stateful workflow implementation with no shared contract. Extract a WorkflowStep or StatefulCommand interface to prevent this pattern from being re-implemented ad-hoc in future multi-step commands.
Zone "cli" has files across 9 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

The bidirectional coupling between cli and unit-analyze (58 outbound + 35 inbound = 93 crossings, finding global-0) is corroborated by cmdAnalyze calling 44 unique functions (finding global-12) and the CLI-boundary pattern analysis (finding global-11). The concrete fix is contained to two files: (1) decompose src/cli/commands/analyze.ts so that cmdAnalyze delegates to focused sub-handlers rather than calling 44 functions directly, and (2) audit src/cli/commands/ for files that import unit-analyze internals directly and route those calls through the existing gateway pattern. No file splits are required — this is import discipline and delegation, not decomposition.

The concrete action to resolve the cli↔unit-analyze bidirectional coupling (58+35 crossings, global-0) is: audit src/cli/commands/ for any file that imports from unit-analyze internals without routing through an explicit gateway, then consolidate those imports into a single cli-side gateway module mirroring the existing hench/src/prd/rex-gateway.ts pattern. The only call-site confirmed to require this treatment is src/cli/commands/analyze.ts (44 outgoing calls to unit-analyze, global-12). No file splits are required.

The bidirectional cli↔unit-analyze coupling is corroborated by four independent findings: global-0 (58+35 crossings), cli[3] and cli[4] (LLM analysis identifying analyze.ts as the locus), and global-12 (cmdAnalyze 44 outgoing calls). This convergence elevates confidence: the problem is real, narrow, and fixable without file splits.

With 24 entry points and 48 files this is the broadest domain zone — health.ts, sync-engine.ts, and analytics.ts are architecturally distinct from tree mutation and could be extracted into focused sub-zones to improve cohesion.
public.ts as an entry point signals this zone defines the package's outward API surface; changes here have downstream impact on hench and web consumers and warrant extra care.
Bidirectional imports with unit-analyze (core→unit-analyze: 67, unit-analyze→core: 18) suggest overlapping responsibilities; introducing explicit shared interface types would clarify the boundary.

67 imports from this zone into unit-analyze combined with 18 reverse imports creates a near-cycle between the two largest zones; shared types should be owned at a lower layer to eliminate the reverse dependency.
sync-engine.ts and analytics.ts appear architecturally distinct from tree mutation operations; extracting them would improve zone cohesion and make the domain boundary clearer.
public.ts acts as the package API gateway — keeping it lean and intentional prevents accidental surface area growth as the zone expands.

prd-tree-lifecycle is the only zone that participates in bidirectional relationships with four separate zones (unit-analyze, cli, store, task-selection) simultaneously — it behaves as the system's load-bearing layer rather than a discrete domain zone.

prd-tree-lifecycle participates in bidirectional coupling with four other zones concurrently; no other zone has this many simultaneous bidirectional relationships. This multi-way entanglement makes it impossible to refactor any one relationship without disturbing the others.

core → cli: 21 imports means the domain layer (prd-tree-lifecycle) has an upward dependency on the CLI layer — an architectural inversion. Domain logic must not import from the CLI surface; the 21 callsites should be audited and eliminated.

The zone conflates at least three architecturally distinct concerns: tree mutation (add, delete, move, merge), lifecycle orchestration (dag, reorganize, health), and telemetry (analytics.ts, code-coverage.ts). The 48-file footprint and multi-way bidirectional coupling with four other zones are direct consequences of this over-aggregation. Extracting analytics.ts and code-coverage.ts into a dedicated sub-zone requires no interface changes and would immediately reduce fan-out.

analytics.ts and code-coverage.ts reside in src/core/ giving them a false membership signal in the domain layer — the directory path implies they are domain tree concerns, but they are telemetry/measurement utilities. Files whose logical purpose contradicts their directory placement create misleading zone membership and make the src/core/ directory ambiguous about its own scope.

src/core/analytics.ts and src/core/code-coverage.ts have misleading placement: the src/core/ directory name implies PRD domain logic, but these files are telemetry utilities. Relocating them to src/telemetry/ or src/metrics/ would disambiguate the core/ directory scope and make their extraction from prd-tree-lifecycle structurally visible without changing any interfaces.

prd-tree-lifecycle (48 files) and unit-analyze (69 files) together represent approximately 50% of all source files concentrated in 2 zones. This asymmetric distribution reduces the zone map's navigational value — zones should ideally be within 2–3x of each other in file count. Splitting prd-tree-lifecycle along its already-identified sub-concerns (tree mutation vs. lifecycle orchestration vs. telemetry) would bring zone sizes closer to the 7–23 file range seen in healthier zones.

src/schema/validate.ts is the sole production file grouped with 15 test files — per the '-tests' naming convention the zone is named after its production domain (schema validation) rather than its test content.
Low cohesion (0.29) and high coupling (0.71) are structurally inherent to E2E test suites, which reach across the entire CLI surface by design rather than focusing on a single module.

The broad command coverage (adapter, analyze, import, init, prune, quiet, recommend, and more) provides valuable regression confidence but also means this zone is a catch-all for full-stack CLI tests.

Cohesion 0.29 / coupling 0.71 exceed warning thresholds but are architecturally expected for E2E suites — the underlying issue is src/schema/validate.ts being pulled into a test-dominated cluster rather than a production one.

src/schema/validate.ts being grouped with E2E tests rather than the core zone suggests it is primarily exercised by tests; verify it is also imported from production paths (e.g., rex init or add commands) to confirm correct architectural placement.
Wide E2E coverage across adapter, analyze, import, init, prune, and recommend commands is a healthy sign of end-to-end test investment in the CLI surface.

schema-validation's sole production file (validate.ts) has no visible unit-test zone pairing — unlike fix-command and task-selection which each have dedicated unit test files in their zone. This suggests validate.ts is exercised only through E2E paths, leaving its internal logic untested at the unit level.

validate.ts has no unit-test counterpart in its zone; coverage arrives only through 15 E2E tests. E2E tests validate observable CLI behavior but do not cover internal validation branches — edge-case schema errors may be invisible until they surface in integration paths.

The schema-validation zone is structurally decoupled from prd-storage-adapters despite validate.ts likely being called during store write paths. If the two are not directly linked in the import graph, schema enforcement may be applied inconsistently across different persistence codepaths.

validate.ts being clustered with E2E tests rather than with prd-storage-adapters or prd-tree-lifecycle indicates the import graph sees it as structurally disconnected from production write paths. If validate.ts is called at store write-time it belongs with prd-storage-adapters; if called at parse-time it belongs in prd-tree-lifecycle. Either placement would give it a unit-test pairing and eliminate the E2E-only coverage gap.

validate.ts is the sole production file in a 15-test-file E2E cluster, with no unit-test zone pairing and zero cross-imports from prd-storage-adapters despite schema enforcement being a write-path concern. The file is structurally misplaced and its internal logic branches are invisible to unit testing.

Coupling score of 0.71 is a measurement artifact: E2E test files are structurally required to import broadly across the CLI surface, so including them in coupling calculations inflates the metric for any test-heavy zone. The single production file (validate.ts) should be evaluated independently of the 15 test files it is co-clustered with.

schema-validation has the worst combined metrics in the codebase (cohesion 0.29, coupling 0.71) yet is not immediately actionable because the metrics reflect test-file inclusion artifacts rather than production coupling — flagging it as a structural risk without qualifying this distinction would lead to misguided refactoring effort.

Zone health metrics for schema-validation are unreliable as structural indicators because 15 of 16 files are E2E tests whose cross-CLI imports are expected behavior, not defects. Exclude E2E test files from zone coupling calculations or track them in a separate test-zone metric to avoid false positive warnings.

validate.ts is named with the 'validate' verb prefix while its zone is named 'schema-validation' — the zone name uses a noun-phrase pattern while the file uses a verb pattern. Align to one convention: either schema-validator.ts (noun) or keep validate.ts and rename the zone to 'schema-validate' for verb-object consistency with 'fix-command'.

insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
The presence of llms.txt and context.md at the root alongside README.md suggests this project treats AI-readable documentation as a first-class artifact alongside human-readable docs.
Zero coupling to any source zone is correct — these files define the project container, not the domain logic.
The vitest.config.ts here likely acts as the root-level test runner configuration; ensure it delegates properly to per-package configs to avoid test isolation gaps.
Perfect cohesion and zero coupling confirm this zone correctly contains only project-level static files with no runtime dependencies.

zones.name: Store
insights: 9 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports

Cohesion of 0.6 is the highest in this batch — the adapter registry, contracts, integration schemas, and sync CLI are tightly unified around a single storage abstraction, making this the healthiest structural unit.
Integration schemas for Jira and Notion follow a clean extensible pattern indexed via integration-schemas/index.ts — adding new integrations requires only a new schema file and registration entry.
src/cli/commands/sync.ts living in this zone rather than the CLI zone creates a minor structural asymmetry; if further CLI commands accumulate here the zone's purpose will blur.
Strong cohesion (0.6) and moderate coupling (0.4) make this the best-balanced zone in the batch — the adapter-registry pattern is working well and is worth replicating elsewhere.
src/cli/commands/sync.ts is co-located with store internals rather than the CLI zone; relocating it to the CLI layer would better reflect the architectural boundary.
Zone "prd-storage-adapters" has files across 8 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

store → unit-analyze: 22 imports means the storage adapter layer has an upward dependency on the analysis layer — a clear architectural inversion. A foundation-layer concern (persistence) must not depend on a higher-layer concern (analysis/domain).

store → unit-analyze: 22 imports is an architectural inversion: the storage layer (expected to be a low-level foundation) imports 22 times from the analysis zone (a higher-level domain concern). This is the most structurally dangerous inversion in the codebase because it ties persistence to domain logic, preventing either from evolving independently.

prd-storage-adapters is cleanly isolated from fix-command and task-selection (zero cross-imports in either direction) — storage concerns are correctly invisible to command-leaf zones, which is a healthy boundary.

src/cli/commands/sync.ts is misplaced inside the storage zone. CLI command files belong in the CLI layer; co-locating commands with their backing domain sets a precedent that would scatter the CLI layer across every domain zone if followed consistently.

prd-storage-adapters is the only zone achieving cohesion ≥ 0.6 with coupling ≤ 0.4, yet no zone in the codebase achieves both high cohesion (>0.6) AND low coupling (<0.3) simultaneously. The absence of a true foundation/types layer means every zone must import peer zones for shared definitions, keeping all coupling floors elevated. Extracting src/schema/index.ts and shared PRD types into a dedicated src/foundation/ module would give every zone a downward-only dependency sink and is the single change most likely to reduce the coupling floor across all zones simultaneously.
Zone "store" has files across 8 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

At cohesion 0.47 and coupling 0.53, this zone sits at the boundary — files here straddle recommendation logic and general analysis utilities, which explains the moderate scores.
The 23 imports from unit-analyze and 12 exports back to unit-analyze suggest this zone is tightly paired with the core analysis pipeline and may benefit from merging or clearer interface contracts.
The recommend/types.ts file should be the stable contract point between this zone and its consumers; verify it doesn't leak implementation details from scanners.ts or conflict-detection.ts.

Cohesion at 0.47 is at the warning threshold — the mix of CLI command (recommend.ts), domain service (create-from-recommendations.ts), and utility files (conflict-detection.ts) suggests this zone blends layers that could be separated.
Bidirectional imports with unit-analyze (12 outbound, 23 inbound) indicate shared state or shared types that haven't been cleanly factored into a neutral boundary module.
Zone "recommendation-scanning" has files across 6 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

Imports from both prd-analysis-core (23 inbound) and core/prd-tree-lifecycle (6 inbound) while exporting back to both (12+4) — this zone is caught between two hubs rather than sitting at a clean layer boundary.

The zone receives more imports than it sends outbound to prd-tree-lifecycle (4 out vs 6 in), suggesting prd-tree-lifecycle treats recommendation logic as a downstream consumer of tree operations rather than a peer service.

recommendation-scanning participates in bidirectional coupling with both prd-analysis-core AND prd-tree-lifecycle simultaneously, making it a bridge zone rather than a leaf — changes in either hub ripple through it.

Four files (acknowledge.ts, extract.ts, reconcile.ts, scanners.ts) live in src/analyze/ but are assigned to recommendation-scanning — they share a directory with prd-analysis-core files, creating a directory-level boundary violation where zone membership cannot be inferred from file location alone.

acknowledge.ts, extract.ts, reconcile.ts, and scanners.ts reside in src/analyze/ alongside prd-analysis-core files but belong to a different zone — zone boundaries are invisible at the filesystem level, making them impossible to enforce via directory-based lint rules.

src/analyze/scanners.ts and src/analyze/extract.ts make direct LLM calls (spawnClaude) while also being consumed by prd-analysis-core — LLM I/O is scattered across two zones in the same directory, preventing either zone from being tested in isolation.

recommendation-scanning is the only zone where BOTH cohesion (0.47) and coupling (0.53) simultaneously cross the warning threshold in opposite directions — insufficient cohesion combined with excess coupling is the definition of a fragile zone, making this the highest-priority zone-level refactoring target ahead of prd-analysis-core.

The verb-gerund naming pattern ('recommendation-scanning') is inconsistent with the noun-phrase pattern used by all other zones ('prd-analysis-core', 'cli-mcp-interface', 'prd-tree-lifecycle', 'prd-storage-adapters') — rename to 'recommendation-engine' or 'analyze-recommendations' to align with zone naming conventions.

recommendation-scanning is the only zone with both cohesion below 0.5 and coupling above 0.5 simultaneously — it is the most fragile zone by definition. Prioritize splitting it before prd-analysis-core refactoring, as its bridging role between two hubs amplifies any change in either.
Zone name uses verb-gerund pattern ('recommendation-scanning') while all peer zones use noun phrases. Rename to 'recommendation-engine' for consistency.
Zone "unit" has files across 6 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

This zone contains unit test files. Coupling to unit-analyze is expected test-to-implementation imports, not a structural defect. Risk metrics derived from test-to-production coupling are false positives under the constraint that test-to-implementation coupling is correct by design.
All metric-derived risk findings are false positives. unit is a test zone; coupling to unit-analyze is intentional test-to-implementation dependency. Zone risk thresholds do not apply.

zones.name: Unit Analyze
insights: 25 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports

At 69 files this is the largest zone by a wide margin, and its 25 entry points confirm it acts as the architectural hub — nearly every other zone depends on it, making it a high-leverage but high-risk area to change.

The presence of both schema/ and analyze/ files in the same zone is appropriate — schema changes and analysis logic are tightly coupled — but the CLI command files (add.ts, smart-add.ts, status.ts, validate.ts) reaching into this zone blurs the domain/presentation boundary.

The consolidation-guard.ts and smart-add-duplicates.ts files suggest deduplication and merge safety are active concerns; verify these utilities have direct unit test coverage rather than relying on integration tests alone.

With 69 files and 25 entry points, this zone is the dependency hub of the system. High fan-in from cli, core, store, and unit zones means changes here have wide blast radius — consider treating public API surface (schema/index.ts, analyze/index.ts) as a formal contract.

CLI command files (add.ts, smart-add.ts, status.ts) co-located with domain logic (decompose.ts, dedupe.ts, diff.ts) mixes presentation and domain layers within a single zone, reducing cohesion clarity.
Zone "prd-analysis-core" has files across 12 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

prd-analysis-core is the only zone that receives imports from every other major source zone (cli: 58, core: 67, unit: 23, store: 22, unit-core: 7) — universal fan-in with no clean downstream consumer confirms it acts as an implicit shared-state module rather than a domain layer.

prd-analysis-core exports back to every zone that imports it (cli: 35, core: 18, unit: 12, store: 8) — every dependency relationship is bidirectional, meaning there is no safe direction to refactor without touching all consumers simultaneously.

prd-analysis-core has zero unidirectional dependencies — all 5 zones that import it also receive imports back, making it impossible to extract or stabilize in isolation. This is the most systemic architectural risk in the codebase.
The store → prd-analysis-core (22) and prd-analysis-core → store (8) cycle means analysis logic and persistence are mutually entangled; neither can be tested or deployed independently.

The src/analyze/llm-bridge.ts and reason.ts files make direct synchronous Claude CLI process spawns (spawnClaude) inside the domain hub — every one of the 177 cross-zone consumers of this zone transitively inherits a hard dependency on Claude CLI availability at runtime, making the zone untestable in isolation without a live Claude process.

src/schema/index.ts is documented as a high-fan-in contract with 71 internal importers yet is classified inside the hub zone rather than as a distinct foundation layer — consumers cannot take a lightweight schema-only dependency without pulling in the full prd-analysis-core zone graph.

The llm-bridge.ts file implements an adapter pattern (configuration, retry, model selection) but lives inside the domain hub rather than in @n-dx/llm-client, duplicating the concern that llm-client already owns.

src/schema/index.ts and src/schema/v1.ts are high-fan-in type-only modules (71 importers) co-located inside the domain hub zone — they should be extracted to a foundation layer so consumers can depend on schema types without coupling to analysis logic.

src/analyze/llm-bridge.ts embeds a direct Claude CLI subprocess adapter inside the domain hub; LLM I/O should be injectable (interface + adapter in llm-client) so analysis logic can be unit-tested without a live Claude process.

src/analyze/reshape-reason.ts is classified in cli-mcp-interface but lives in src/analyze/ and imports exclusively from other prd-analysis-core files (reason.ts, core/reshape.ts) — it is a misplaced file that belongs in prd-analysis-core.

consolidation-guard.ts and smart-add-duplicates.ts enforce invariants at runtime in the service layer — these guards protect against data corruption that TypeScript type constraints at the schema layer could prevent statically. Runtime guards are a sign that the schema types are insufficiently narrow.

The 25 entry points in prd-analysis-core map to no documented public contract — any consumer can import any internal file directly, and nothing distinguishes stable public API from internal implementation details. Without a formal public.ts, the 'public surface' grows implicitly with every new file added.

consolidation-guard.ts and smart-add-duplicates.ts are runtime defensive guards against data shape violations. Tightening the schema types in src/schema/v1.ts (narrower union types, branded IDs, non-optional fields) would move these checks to compile time and eliminate the need for the runtime guards entirely.

25 entry points with no public.ts means the public API surface grows by default with every new file. Add a src/public.ts barrel that explicitly controls what is exported, matching the pattern used by other packages in this monorepo.
Zone "unit-analyze" has files across 12 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

fix-command does not appear as a source in the cross-zone import table, confirming it is a pure consumer with no dependents — the zone is a leaf node in the dependency graph, which is the ideal topology for a command module.

fix-command is a dependency-graph leaf: zero outbound cross-zone exports, imports only from prd-analysis-core. This is the correct topology for a command implementation and should be the reference pattern for other command zones.

fix-command and task-selection both have coupling scores near the 0.6 warning threshold partly because unit test files that import the module under test mechanically contribute to coupling. Near-threshold coupling (0.6) in a 4-file zone will likely cross the warning boundary when additional test cases are added, since unit test imports count toward coupling. task-selection and fix-command are both dependency-graph leaves with no downstream consumers — these two zones define the correct command-module pattern (consume, never export to other zones) and their structure should guide future command additions.

The most significant architectural issue in this batch is the bidirectional coupling between cli and prd-analysis-core (93 total cross-imports in both directions) and between core and prd-analysis-core (85 total) — these pairs behave more like merged zones than layered ones.

The prd-analysis-core zone is the system's gravitational center with the highest fan-in; architectural improvements should prioritize stabilizing its public API surface before other refactors, as changes there ripple across every other zone.
The two data-only zones (.rex, root) have perfect cohesion and zero coupling, confirming that the import-graph analysis correctly excludes non-source artifacts from the dependency topology.

The heaviest cross-zone dependency pair (core → unit-analyze: 67, unit-analyze → core: 18) forms a near-cycle between the two largest zones; introducing shared interface types at a lower layer is the highest-priority structural improvement.

The cli zone is a high-traffic hub: it sends 58 imports to unit-analyze and 36 to core while receiving 35 and 21 back respectively — bidirectional CLI↔domain dependencies risk cascading breakage on CLI signature changes.

prd-storage-adapters is the only zone in this batch with cohesion ≥ 0.6 and coupling ≤ 0.4; its adapter-registry and explicit-contracts patterns are worth promoting as the reference architecture for other zones.

unit-cli and unit-core both mix production source files with unit tests at near-warning coupling levels; this co-location is intentional for locality but risks tooling misclassifying production files as test-only if zone names are not carefully maintained.

src/schema/validate.ts orphaned into an E2E-dominated zone suggests the import graph sees schema validation as primarily test-exercised; confirming production callsites exist is important before any planned refactor of the schema layer.

Four of five source zones have coupling between 0.48 and 0.53, clustering tightly around the 0.5 midpoint. This suggests the community detection algorithm found natural clusters but the codebase has not yet enforced strict layering — zones are cohesive enough to be meaningful but still leak across boundaries.

The unit-analyze zone's 25 entry points make it the de-facto public API of the rex package. Without explicit access control (e.g. barrel exports or a formal public.ts), consumers can import any internal file directly, increasing the risk of unintended coupling.

A near-cycle exists between the prd-tree-lifecycle and PRD Analysis Core zones (67 + 18 bidirectional imports); shared types should be extracted to a lower-level module so the dependency flows strictly downward.

The CLI & MCP Interface zone receives 35 imports from PRD Analysis Core while also sending 58 back, creating a bidirectional dependency that should be resolved by clarifying which zone owns shared command orchestration contracts.

The prd-storage-adapters zone's structural health metrics are the strongest across all zones; its patterns (adapter registry, explicit contracts, integration schema index) serve as a reference for refactoring higher-coupling zones.

The '-tests' suffix naming convention is at risk from zones like unit-cli and unit-core whose algorithmic IDs suggest test content but contain production source files; the renamed zone IDs (fix-command, task-selection) correctly surface production purpose.
src/analyze/ contains files from 3 zones (cli-mcp-interface, prd-analysis-core, recommendation-scanning) — consider grouping into subdirectories to clarify architectural boundaries

No zone in the codebase has exclusively unidirectional dependencies — every major zone participates in at least one bidirectional relationship. This is a systemic pattern of layering breakdown, not a set of isolated violations.

The store zone (cohesion 0.6) is structurally the healthiest zone and the only candidate to serve as a stable foundation layer, but its bidirectional coupling with both prd-analysis-core and core prevents it from fulfilling that role without first extracting shared types downward.

The e2e zone (cohesion 0.29, 16 files) does not appear in cross-zone import counts as an exporter, confirming it is correctly one-directional — it imports from production zones but nothing imports from it. This is the only clean dependency direction in the entire graph.

The unit-cli zone (4 files, cohesion 0.4) is too small to represent a meaningful architectural boundary; at this size, Louvain community detection likely grouped stragglers rather than a coherent subsystem — consider merging into cli-mcp-interface.

A three-tier transitive cycle exists: cli-mcp-interface → store → prd-analysis-core → cli-mcp-interface (35 back). This closed loop means any change that crosses a zone boundary can propagate around the full cycle, eliminating the blast-radius containment that zone separation is meant to provide.

The prd-tree-lifecycle zone (core, 48 files) is the second-largest zone and sends the single highest import count in the graph (67 to prd-analysis-core) while receiving 18 back — this pair alone accounts for 85 cross-zone crossings and represents the highest-volume near-cycle.

The codebase has two confirmed architectural inversions: (1) domain core → CLI layer (21 imports) and (2) storage layer → analysis layer (22 imports). Both violate strict downward dependency flow and represent the highest-priority structural fixes ahead of any cohesion improvements.

Leaf-zone pattern: fix-command and task-selection are the only zones with zero outbound cross-zone exports. All other zones participate in bidirectional coupling. Enforcing the leaf pattern (command zones consume, never export) for all CLI command zones would eliminate most of the bidirectional entanglement in the cli↔core and cli↔unit-analyze pairs.

The cross-zone import table reveals that unit-analyze is the most imported zone in the system (receiving from core:67, cli:58, unit:23, store:22, unit-core:7) with a combined fan-in of 177 imports — nearly double the next highest. This concentration makes unit-analyze the single highest-risk zone for cascading breakage; any API change there propagates to every other zone simultaneously.

Three zones (fix-command, task-selection, schema-validation) are structurally isolated from prd-storage-adapters with zero cross-imports in either direction — confirming that the storage abstraction is correctly invisible to command-layer and schema concerns. This clean boundary should be explicitly documented and enforced to prevent future drift.

The overall dependency topology has a missing strict-layering contract: with inversions at both the CLI-domain boundary and the storage-analysis boundary, the four-tier hierarchy described in CLAUDE.md (Orchestration → Execution → Domain → Foundation) is not enforced in the import graph. Adding an ESLint import-boundary rule (e.g. eslint-plugin-boundaries) would make the intended layering mechanically enforceable.

Every zone boundary in the source graph is bidirectional. The architecture has no strict layering — it operates as a mesh. Introducing even one unidirectional interface contract (e.g. a shared-types foundation layer below prd-analysis-core) would break all existing cycles simultaneously.

The three hubs (prd-analysis-core, cli-mcp-interface, prd-tree-lifecycle) collectively account for over 85% of all cross-zone imports. Stabilizing these three zones' public API surfaces would reduce systemic coupling more than any other single intervention.

Two architectural inversions coexist: core→cli (21 imports) and store→unit-analyze (22 imports). Together they mean both the domain layer and the storage layer have upward dependencies, which structurally prevents the four-tier hierarchy from being enforced. Resolving these two inversions should precede any other zone-level refactoring.

unit-analyze has a fan-in of 177 cross-zone imports (core:67, cli:58, unit:23, store:22, unit-core:7) with no other zone close to this concentration. It is a de-facto shared kernel without a formal interface contract — any rename or signature change in unit-analyze requires coordinated updates across all five consumer zones simultaneously.

The two smallest command zones (fix-command: 4 files, task-selection: 7 files) are the only dependency-graph leaves and have the cleanest structural metrics relative to their coupling scores. Zone size correlates inversely with bidirectional entanglement in this codebase — smaller, focused zones naturally avoid the multi-directional coupling seen in prd-tree-lifecycle (48 files) and unit-analyze (69 files).

store is the only zone with cohesion ≥ 0.6 and is not a hub — it is the reference architecture for the rest of the codebase, but its structural health is undermined by being pulled into bidirectional cycles with both core and prd-analysis-core.

The src/analyze/ directory hosts files from three separate zones (prd-analysis-core, cli-mcp-interface, recommendation-scanning) — no directory-based import boundary rule (eslint-plugin-boundaries, barrel-only imports) can be enforced here because the zone split cuts across a single directory.

Direct Claude CLI process spawning (spawnClaude via llm-bridge.ts) is distributed across at least two zones (prd-analysis-core, recommendation-scanning) without a shared injectable interface — the @n-dx/llm-client package exists for exactly this purpose but is not used by rex's internal analysis layer, creating a duplicated LLM adapter outside the foundation layer.

src/schema/index.ts has 71 internal importers spanning every zone in the package yet is embedded inside the largest hub zone — it is the de-facto foundation contract for the entire rex package and should be the first module extracted when breaking up prd-analysis-core, as doing so would immediately clarify which consumers depend on types only vs. analysis behavior.

The three files with direct LLM calls (reason.ts, guided.ts, decompose.ts, reshape-reason.ts, scanners.ts, extract.ts) are spread across two zones with no shared interface — introducing an LLMAdapter interface in the foundation layer and injecting it would allow all analysis and recommendation logic to be tested with a stub, eliminating the current hard dependency on a live Claude process in the domain hub.

The two confirmed architectural inversions (core→cli: 21, store→unit-analyze: 22) occur at adjacent tiers in the four-tier hierarchy. Because they coexist, neither the domain tier nor the storage tier can be independently refactored: changes propagate both upward (into CLI) and upward-diagonal (into analysis) at the same time. Fixing one inversion without the other leaves the hierarchy still broken.

The transitive cycle cli→store→prd-analysis-core→cli is a closed dependency loop. Any breaking API change in any member zone requires coordinated updates across all three simultaneously. This single cycle is the structural root cause of the high observed coupling scores and should be the primary target of interface-extraction work before zone-level cohesion improvements are attempted.

All architectural layering rules documented in CLAUDE.md are convention-only — no eslint-plugin-boundaries rules, no TypeScript path restrictions, and no CI gate enforces the four-tier hierarchy. The two inversions and the transitive cycle will silently reappear after any refactor without mechanical enforcement.

LLM subprocess calls (spawnClaude) are implemented directly in prd-analysis-core and recommendation-scanning rather than through the @n-dx/llm-client foundation package — the foundation layer that exists for this purpose is bypassed, making the entire domain layer untestable without a live Claude CLI.

src/analyze/ contains files from three different zones — directory-based architectural enforcement (import boundary linting, barrel-only access) is structurally impossible in this directory until files are reorganized into zone-aligned subdirectories.

The transitive dependency cycle cli-mcp-interface → prd-storage-adapters → prd-analysis-core → cli-mcp-interface forms a closed loop. No zone in this cycle can be refactored, versioned, or extracted independently — any breaking change requires coordinated updates across all three zones. This is the most severe structural issue in the codebase.

No mechanical import boundary enforcement exists (no eslint-plugin-boundaries, no TypeScript path aliases with restrictions). The two confirmed inversions (core→cli, store→unit-analyze) will recur silently after any refactor. Adding eslint-plugin-boundaries rules aligned with the four-tier hierarchy described in CLAUDE.md would make the layering contract durable and catch future violations at CI time.

The gateway module pattern mandated in CLAUDE.md for cross-PACKAGE imports has no equivalent discipline for cross-ZONE imports within rex. While inter-package dependencies must pass through a single gateway file, any file in rex can import any other file across zone boundaries without restriction — the same architectural discipline that makes inter-package coupling auditable is absent at the intra-package level.

The cross-zone import table uses shorthand aliases (unit-analyze, core, cli, store, unit-core) that do not match the canonical zone IDs (prd-analysis-core, prd-tree-lifecycle, cli-mcp-interface, prd-storage-adapters) — there is no documented alias→zone-id mapping, making the cross-zone import data ambiguous to readers who encounter only one representation.

Exactly two zones cross both warning thresholds simultaneously (cohesion < 0.5 AND coupling > 0.5): recommendation-scanning (0.47/0.53) and cli-mcp-interface (0.49/0.51). All other source zones have at least one metric within safe range. These two zones should be treated as a co-refactoring unit since they share bidirectional imports and both act as bridges between the major hubs.

The intra-package zone structure has no mechanical enforcement equivalent to eslint-plugin-boundaries or TypeScript path aliases — zone membership is a post-hoc classification by the analysis tool, not a property enforced at authorship time. This means zone cohesion metrics will silently degrade after every commit without any developer feedback loop.

No zone in the codebase achieves both high cohesion (>0.6) and low coupling (<0.3) simultaneously. The minimum achievable coupling floor is constrained by the absence of a shared types/foundation layer — every zone must reach into peer zones to obtain shared type definitions. Creating a src/foundation/ or src/types/ module as a dependency sink below all current zones is the prerequisite for any zone achieving clean unidirectional metrics.

Zone naming uses three incompatible conventions across the codebase: verb-object (fix-command), noun-phrase (task-selection, prd-tree-lifecycle, prd-storage-adapters), and domain-layer compound (schema-validation). No single pattern governs zone naming, making the zone map harder to scan and the zone-naming algorithm's output less predictable for future zones. Adopting one convention (recommend: noun-phrase matching the production domain) and documenting it would make zone names self-consistent.

Coupling metrics that include test files alongside production files systematically inflate coupling scores for zones with thorough unit test coverage. The three test-heavy zones (schema-validation: 15 tests/1 prod, task-selection: 5 tests/2 prod, fix-command: 2 tests/2 prod) have their highest coupling contributions from test-to-production imports within the zone, not from cross-zone production dependencies. A separate test-coupling metric tracked independently of production coupling would make structural warnings actionable rather than noise.

The five core production zones span coupling scores from 0.4 to 0.71 with no zone below 0.4 — the lower bound is structurally imposed by the absence of a foundation layer, not by implementation choices. This means zone-level refactoring alone cannot move any zone below the current coupling floor; architectural extraction of a shared-types layer is the only intervention that can shift the floor downward for multiple zones simultaneously.

Establish an intra-package gateway convention mirroring the inter-package gateway pattern in CLAUDE.md: each zone exposes a single index.ts barrel, and all cross-zone imports within rex must go through that barrel. This makes zone API surfaces explicit and enforceable by lint rule without requiring a full monorepo split.

Document the alias-to-zone-ID mapping (unit-analyze=prd-analysis-core, core=prd-tree-lifecycle, store=prd-storage-adapters, cli=cli-mcp-interface) in the sourcevision CONTEXT.md or zone metadata. Without this mapping, cross-zone import counts in reports are ambiguous.

The two dual-threshold fragile zones (recommendation-scanning, cli-mcp-interface) share a bidirectional import relationship. Refactor them together in a single pass rather than sequentially: extract their shared types into a neutral boundary module, then sever the bidirectional links from both sides simultaneously.

Standardize zone naming to a single noun-phrase convention matching the production domain purpose. Current mixed conventions (verb-object, noun-phrase, domain-layer compound) make the zone map inconsistent and reduce its utility as a navigation aid. Proposed: fix-command → 'prd-fix', task-selection → 'task-selection' (already correct), schema-validation → 'schema-validation' (already noun-phrase, but the 'validation' suffix differs from 'fix-command' verb pattern). Document the chosen convention in CLAUDE.md alongside the zone topology rules.

The zone health scoring model (cohesion/coupling) does not distinguish test-file imports from production-file imports. This causes systematic false positives for zones with strong unit test coverage. Before acting on any coupling warning for zones containing unit or E2E test files, decompose the coupling score into test-only and production-only contributions to confirm the warning is from production coupling rather than test coverage density.

The four-tier hierarchy (Orchestration → Execution → Domain → Foundation) documented in CLAUDE.md has no corresponding zone below the current domain tier to absorb shared types. The missing Foundation zone means cross-domain type sharing is achieved by importing from peer or upper zones, which is the structural root cause of all confirmed architectural inversions. Adding a src/foundation/ zone as the lowest tier — containing only types, constants, and zero logic — would make the four-tier model mechanically enforceable with import boundary linting.

The CLI layer is the primary architectural risk area: findings 0 (bidirectional coupling involving cli-mcp-interface), 1 (constants hub imported by 6 zones), 7 (god main with 48 calls), and 8 (60-call coupling in CLI commands) collectively describe a CLI layer that has accumulated domain logic it should not own — this convergence of independent signals is more significant than any single finding alone.

High fan-in on foundational modules (schema/index.ts at 22 importers, walkTree at 29 callers) reflects healthy architecture for barrel exports and core tree utilities — these are intentional public API surfaces, not hotspots requiring remediation. Finding 3's warning severity is inconsistent with this pattern.

The 3 at-risk zones (task-selection, fix-command, recommendation-scanning) likely share a root cause: CLI command handler logic mixed with domain logic, which is the same structural pressure driving the god-main and bidirectional coupling findings. The co-occurrence across findings 0, 6, and 7 suggests a single systemic issue rather than three independent problems.

Zone boundaries do not align with directory boundaries (finding 4: src/analyze/ spans 3 zones), which reduces the value of zone metrics as architectural signals for that subtree — cohesion/coupling scores for cli-mcp-interface and prd-analysis-core may be artifacts of this misalignment rather than genuine design problems.
src/analyze/ contains files from 3 zones (cli, unit, unit-analyze) — consider grouping into subdirectories to clarify architectural boundaries

Finding 3 assigns 'warning' severity to src/schema/index.ts for 22-file fan-in, but this file is a barrel export (index.ts) whose entire purpose is aggregating and re-exporting schema types. High fan-in is the correct outcome for this pattern. Finding 5 correctly notes 'high fan-in expected for this module type' for walkTree — the same logic applies to schema/index.ts. The warning on finding 3 is unsupported; info is appropriate.

Finding 5 simultaneously notes '(high fan-in expected for this module type)' and then suggests 'consider if responsibilities can be narrowed' — these are internally contradictory. If high fan-in is expected for a core tree utility, no refactoring signal follows from it. The suggestion to narrow responsibilities is not supported by the finding's own qualifier and should be disregarded absent corroborating evidence.

For the bidirectional coupling between cli-mcp-interface and prd-analysis-core (finding 0), corroborated by src/analyze/ spanning 3 zones (finding 4): introduce a thin shared-types or analyze-contracts module within src/analyze/ that both zones import. This converts bidirectional coupling into two unidirectional dependencies on a neutral module, without requiring file decomposition above the 2x threshold.

For the 3 at-risk zones (task-selection, fix-command, recommendation-scanning) in finding 6: each zone's high coupling likely reflects CLI handler code importing domain modules directly. Audit each zone for files that mix argument-parsing/output-formatting logic with domain calls, and move domain calls behind the existing gateway pattern already used in hench and web packages. This is a targeted change to existing files, not a structural reorganization.

Three independent findings (0, 6, 7) converge on the CLI-to-domain boundary as the primary structural risk: the cli-mcp-interface zone has bidirectional coupling with prd-analysis-core (93 total crossings), the main() dispatcher calls 48 unique functions, and 2 of the 3 at-risk zones (fix-command, task-selection) are CLI-adjacent. The actionable fix is to enforce a strict dispatch boundary in src/cli/index.ts: main() should delegate to per-command entry points that own their own domain calls, eliminating the fan-out that produces both the god-function metric and the cross-zone coupling.

The zone detection algorithm is not test-aware. Three zones (unit, unit-cli, unit-core) are composed of unit test files, yet their coupling metrics are evaluated identically to production code zones. This causes all inter-zone coupling from test files to their implementation targets to register as structural risk, inflating risk scores and generating false-positive catastrophic/critical ratings. The two genuinely critical findings (unit-cli finding 2, unit-core finding 2) should be treated as info once test-zone status is recognized.

After discounting test-zone false positives, all remaining elevated-severity findings converge on a single production-code root cause: the CLI layer's coupling to the domain. Findings global-0 (bidirectional cli↔unit-analyze, 93 crossings), global-11 (CLI-to-domain boundary as primary risk), global-12 (cmdAnalyze god function, 44 calls), and global-8 (bidirectional cli-mcp-interface↔prd-analysis-core) are four independent signals pointing to the same structural problem. This convergence elevates the CLI coupling issue above any other concern in the codebase.

Contradiction resolved: global finding 3 (warning on src/schema/index.ts for 22-file fan-in) directly contradicts global finding 5 (which correctly identifies schema/index.ts as a barrel export where high fan-in is the intended outcome). Finding 5 is correct; finding 3's warning severity is unsupported. The schema barrel export is a hub by design, not by accident, and is correctly comparable to the walkTree pattern acknowledged in finding 9.

Contradiction resolved: global finding 9 notes walkTree's high fan-in is 'expected for this module type' and then immediately suggests narrowing its responsibilities — a self-contradictory prescription. Global finding 6 correctly identifies this contradiction. The suggestion to narrow walkTree's responsibilities has no independent evidentiary basis and should not drive action.

Global finding 10 ('3 zones exceed architectural risk thresholds — mandatory refactoring recommended') names unit-core, unit-cli, and unit. All three are test zones whose coupling metrics are expected test-to-implementation imports. The mandatory refactoring recommendation is a false positive produced by applying production-code thresholds to test code without adjustment. This finding is correctly downgraded to info.

The store zone's 9 entry points (finding store-0, warning) lacks corroboration from any other finding. A single heuristic observation of a wide API surface, without evidence of problematic coupling or god-function patterns, does not meet the threshold for a warning under the evaluation constraints. This finding should be treated as informational pending additional evidence.

The size imbalance between unit-analyze (69 files) and the smallest zones (4 files) partly reflects the test-zone decomposition issue: test files for analyze, cli, and core have been split into separate zones, while the implementation code for analyze remains unified. The imbalance metric (global finding 2) is therefore a measurement artifact of zone boundaries, not an independent signal of problematic structure.

The e2e zone (cohesion 1, coupling 0) and the .rex zone (cohesion 1, coupling 0) represent the healthiest structural units in the codebase. Their self-contained topology confirms that full decoupling is achievable within this project's conventions and should be the target model for refactoring the CLI zone.

Zone risk metrics are computed uniformly across test and production zones, causing test-to-implementation coupling to register as structural risk. Three zones — unit, unit-cli, unit-core — carry inflated or false-positive risk scores solely because their test files import the modules they test. Any automated risk threshold that fires on these zones should be suppressed or filtered when zone membership is predominantly test files.

Four independent findings (global-0, global-8, global-11, global-12) converge on the CLI-to-domain coupling as the sole high-confidence structural problem in production code. The actionable surface is narrow: src/cli/commands/analyze.ts (cmdAnalyze, 44 outgoing calls) and any cli-zone files that import unit-analyze internals without going through an explicit gateway. Addressing these two points eliminates the bidirectional coupling and resolves the god-function metric simultaneously.

All three zones flagged as exceeding architectural risk thresholds (unit, unit-cli, unit-core) are test zones. Their coupling to unit-analyze is test-to-subject coupling, expected and correct by design. Risk threshold logic must filter zones where the majority of files are test files before emitting warnings or criticals.

The production structural problem is bounded and well-evidenced: four independent findings (global-0, global-8, zone cli/3, global-12) converge on src/cli/commands/analyze.ts as the single call-site driving the cli↔unit-analyze bidirectional coupling. The fix is import discipline via the existing gateway pattern — no file decomposition is warranted.

src/schema/index.ts accumulating 22 importers (global-4) is consistent with a foundational type-hub role in a layered architecture. High fan-in at the schema layer is a positive signal of reuse, not a structural risk.

The e2e and .rex zones achieving cohesion=1 coupling=0 (global-3) demonstrate that the gateway pattern already embedded in this project reliably eliminates cross-zone drift. They are the correct target model for the cli zone refactor.

Across all 35 findings, zero problems are identified in the store, core, or unit-analyze zones themselves. Structural issues are entirely an artifact of (a) test zone metric inflation and (b) one CLI command file with unmediated upstream imports.

global-11 (src/cli/commands/prune.ts → src/cli/output.ts, 60 cross-file calls, unidirectional) is a single heuristic finding with no corroborating evidence. Unidirectional high-call-count to a dedicated output module is consistent with a well-factored caller/renderer split. There is no evidence of tangled responsibilities and no basis for a structural concern at this time.

Zone risk findings for unit-cli (critical, score 0.75), unit-core (critical, score 0.83), and unit (warning, score 0.67) directly contradict global finding global-9, which correctly identifies these as false positives caused by test-to-implementation coupling. The zone-level severity ratings are incorrect; global-9 is correct. Automated risk thresholds should be gated on zone composition (test-file ratio) before firing.

The zone risk scoring algorithm is not test-aware. Zones with a majority of test files (unit, unit-cli, unit-core) receive inflated coupling and cohesion scores because unit-test-to-implementation imports are counted as structural coupling. This produces false-positive critical and warning ratings. Risk thresholds should gate on test-file ratio before emitting zone-level risk findings.

Exactly one high-confidence structural problem exists in production code: the bidirectional cli↔unit-analyze coupling centered on src/cli/commands/analyze.ts. It is corroborated by four independent findings from two separate analysis passes (heuristic and LLM). Every other warning-level finding in this report is either a false positive (test zones), a single-source heuristic with no corroboration, or an expected pattern (unidirectional caller/renderer coupling).

The e2e zone (cohesion 1, coupling 0) and .rex zone (cohesion 1, coupling 0) are the structural health targets for this codebase. Full decoupling has already been demonstrated within this project's conventions and should guide refactoring goals for the cli zone.

Wide API surface warnings (unit-analyze 25 entry points, core 24 entry points, cli 13 entry points) are single-source heuristic observations with no corroborating coupling or quality evidence. They are informational signals requiring no action unless combined with evidence of instability or change amplification.

Global finding 9 (warning: three zones exceed architectural risk thresholds, mandatory refactoring recommended before further development) directly contradicts global finding 7 (info: risk scores for unit, unit-cli, unit-core are false positives caused by test-to-implementation coupling). Global finding 7, corroborated by the constraint in IMPORTANT CONSTRAINTS prohibiting escalation of test-to-implementation coupling, is correct. Global finding 9 should be suppressed for all three named zones; its mandatory refactoring recommendation is unwarranted.

Global finding 13 (warning: src/cli/commands/prune.ts → src/cli/output.ts are tightly coupled, 60 cross-file calls, 'consider extracting shared interface or merging') is contradicted by global finding 4 (info: unidirectional high-call-count to a dedicated output module is consistent with a well-factored caller/renderer split, no evidence of tangled responsibilities). Global finding 4 is correct: unidirectional dependency from a command handler to a dedicated renderer is a clean architectural pattern. Global finding 13 carries no corroborating evidence and its severity should be info, not warning.

The analysis pipeline should suppress zone-level risk findings (coupling threshold, cohesion threshold, risk score) when the zone's file membership is predominantly test files. The current three false-positive critical/warning zone ratings (unit, unit-cli, unit-core) stem entirely from this gap. A test-file ratio check (e.g. suppress if >50% of zone files match *.test.ts or tests/ path prefix) before emitting zone risk findings would eliminate recurring false positives in future passes without losing signal on production zones.

The sole production-code refactoring with multi-source corroboration is: create src/cli/commands/analyze-gateway.ts consolidating all outgoing calls from cmdAnalyze (src/cli/commands/analyze.ts, 44 outgoing calls to unit-analyze) into a single re-export gateway following the established hench/src/prd/rex-gateway.ts pattern. Additionally audit src/cli/commands/ for any other files importing unit-analyze internals directly and route those through the same gateway. This resolves the bidirectional cli↔unit-analyze coupling (58+35 crossings) and the cmdAnalyze god-function metric simultaneously, with no file splits required.

Four independent findings (global-0, global-8, global-11, global-12) converge on the CLI-to-domain coupling as the sole high-confidence structural problem in production code. The actionable surface is narrow: src/cli/commands/analyze.ts (cmdAnalyze, 44 outgoing calls) and any cli-zone files that import unit-analyze internals without going through an explicit gateway. Addressing these two points eliminates the bidirectional coupling and resolves the god-function metric simultaneously.

The sole production-code refactoring with multi-source corroboration is: create src/cli/commands/analyze-gateway.ts consolidating all outgoing calls from cmdAnalyze (src/cli/commands/analyze.ts, 44 outgoing calls to unit-analyze) into a single re-export gateway following the established hench/src/prd/rex-gateway.ts pattern. Additionally audit src/cli/commands/ for any other files importing unit-analyze internals directly and route those through the same gateway. This resolves the bidirectional cli↔unit-analyze coupling (58+35 crossings) and the cmdAnalyze god-function metric simultaneously, with no file splits required.

Zone risk metrics are computed uniformly across test and production zones, causing test-to-implementation coupling to register as structural risk. Three zones — unit, unit-cli, unit-core — carry inflated or false-positive risk scores solely because their test files import the modules they test. Any automated risk threshold that fires on these zones should be suppressed or filtered when zone membership is predominantly test files.
